title: Quiz: Path Integral Methods
description: Test your understanding of path integral molecular dynamics

questions.question: What is a ring polymer in path integral methods?

options.text: A set of P classical beads connected by springs representing one quantum particle

questions.explanation: In path integral methods, each quantum particle is mapped to P classical 'beads' connected by harmonic springs in a ring (periodic boundary). This classical ring polymer system has the same partition function as the original quantum system.

questions.question: For which atoms are nuclear quantum effects most significant?

options.text: Heavy atoms like gold and lead
Light atoms like hydrogen and helium

questions.explanation: Nuclear quantum effects scale inversely with mass. Light atoms like hydrogen have longer de Broglie wavelengths, making quantum delocalization and tunneling significant even at room temperature.

questions.question: What is the main difference between PIMD and RPMD?

options.text: PIMD gives thermodynamics, RPMD gives approximate dynamics
RPMD is classical, PIMD is quantum
They are the same method with different names

questions.explanation: PIMD samples the quantum Boltzmann distribution for thermodynamic properties. RPMD uses the ring polymer dynamics to approximate real-time quantum dynamics, useful for reaction rates and transport.

questions.question: What determines the number of beads P needed in path integral simulations?

options.text: The size of the simulation box

questions.explanation: The number of beads must satisfy ℏωₚ >> kT where ωₚ = √P kT/ℏ. Lower temperatures and lighter masses require more beads for convergence.

questions.question: The de Broglie wavelength of a particle increases with decreasing mass.
questions.explanation: True. λ_dB = h/√(2πmkT), so smaller mass m gives larger wavelength. This is why hydrogen (light) has significant quantum delocalization while heavier atoms behave more classically.

questions.question: What is the centroid in path integral methods?

options.text: The heaviest bead in the ring polymer
The average position of all beads in the ring polymer
The geometric center of the simulation box

questions.explanation: The centroid r̄ = (1/P)Σⱼrⱼ is the average position of all P beads. It behaves like a classical particle and is used in centroid molecular dynamics for spectroscopic calculations.

questions.question: Why do kinetic isotope effects provide evidence for nuclear quantum effects?

options.text: Isotopes have different numbers of electrons
Heavier isotopes tunnel less, affecting reaction rates

questions.explanation: H/D kinetic isotope effects arise because hydrogen tunnels more than deuterium (being lighter). Large KIE values (>7) indicate significant tunneling contributions that classical theory cannot explain.

questions.question: Which are manifestations of nuclear quantum effects? (Select all)
questions.explanation: Zero-point energy, tunneling, and nuclear delocalization are all nuclear quantum effects. Electron correlation is an electronic quantum effect, not a nuclear one.
